zh.San Severino, Campania, Italy: 義大利坎帕尼亞薩利爾諾爾 迪 布爾黑利亞三 史華連奴
zh.lifeSummary: 當Giacomo Sanseverino  conte di Tricarico於大約1170年出生在義大利坎帕尼亞薩利爾諾爾 迪 布爾黑利亞三 史華連奴時，他的父親Roger le Vieux Sanseverino Conte di Tricarico16歲，母親Rogasia de Tricarico16歲。他與Maria Albiria de Sicile結婚。他們至少是1個兒子的父母。他去世於1216年之前在義大利普格利亞。

zh-hans.San Severino, Campania, Italy: 意大利坎帕尼亞薩利爾諾爾 迪 布爾黑利亞三 史華連奴
zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Giacomo Sanseverino  conte di Tricarico于大约1170年出生在意大利坎帕尼亞薩利爾諾爾 迪 布爾黑利亞三 史華連奴时，他的父亲Roger le Vieux Sanseverino Conte di Tricarico16岁，母亲Rogasia de Tricarico16岁。他与Maria Albiria de Sicile结婚。他们至少是1个儿子的父母。他去世于1216年之前在意大利普格利亞。

nameMeanings.name: Sanseverino
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Cono, Enrico, Rocco, Canio, Giuseppe, Vito.

Italian (mainly Naples): habitational name from San Severino Lucano in Potenza, San Severino Marche in Macerata, or Mercato San Severino in Salerno, all so named from the dedication of their churches to Saint Severinus (see   Severin  ).

variants.name: Severin
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Christophe, Florien, Francois, Marcel, Monique, Olivier, Patrice.

German, Swedish, Italian (Veneto), Czech, Croatian, and Romanian; Ukrainian (standard transliteration Severyn); French (Séverin); Slovak (Severín): from a personal name, Latin Severinus, from the same root as Severus (see Severe ). This was borne by several early Christian saints, including bishops of Trier (2nd century), Cologne (4th century), and Bordeaux (5th century), and hermits of Paris (6th century) and Tivoli (8th century), as well as a 5th-century apostle of Austria.
Jewish (Ashkenazic): shortened form of Severinovsky, a habitational name for someone from Severinovka in Ukraine.

nameMeanings.name: conte
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Mario, Angelo, Salvatore, Antonio, Rocco, Carmine, Pasquale, Luigi, Silvio, Silverio, Giuseppe, Sal, Alfonso, Biagio, Carlo, Emilio, Fernando, Gerardo.

Italian: from the title of rank conte ‘count’, from Latin comes, genitive comitis ‘companion’. Probably in this sense (and in the Late Latin sense of ‘traveling companion’), it was a medieval personal name. As a title it was no doubt applied ironically as a nickname for someone with airs and graces or simply as a metonymic occupational name for someone in the service of a count. Compare Lo Conte .
Spanish: variant of   Conde   ‘count’ (see also 1 above).

variants.name: Conti
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Salvatore, Antonio, Gino, Carlo, Domenic, Rocco, Sal, Aldo, Carmela, Enrico, Guido.
Italian: patronymic or plural form of   Conte  .

variants.name: Conde
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Carlos, Manuel, Luis, Juan, Pedro, Raul, Jorge, Ruben, Angel, Cesar, Fernando.  Portuguese Goncalo, Manoel.
Spanish and Portuguese: nickname from the title of rank conde ‘count’, a derivative of Latin comes, comitis ‘companion’. See Conte .
English (Shropshire): variant of Cundy , a cognate of 3 below, or Cound, a habitational name from Cound in Shropshire.

variants.name: Contessa
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Carmela, Gaspare, Rocco, Santo.
Italian:
from contessa ‘countess’, feminine form of Conte .

variants.name: Contino
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Amato, Sal, Vito, Anella, Carmelo, Carmine, Domiano, Luigi, Pasquale, Salvator, Salvatore, Vincenza.
Italian: from a diminutive of conte ‘count’ (see Conte ).

variants.name: Lo Conte
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Rocco, Vito, Angelo, Biagio, Carmine, Francesco, Gino, Manlio, Pasquale, Vincenza.
Italian (southern; also Loconte): variant of Conte , with the addition of the definite article lo.

variants.name: Del Conte
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Dario, Rocco, Sal, Americo, Claudio, Domenic, Estanislao, Luciano, Mario, Nevio.
Italian (mainly Tuscany): literally ‘of or belonging to the count’(see   Conte  ), hence a name denoting the servant or possibly theson of a count or a patronymic from the nickname.

nameMeanings.name: di
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Chinese:

Mandarin form of the surname 狄, a term referring to the tribes in ancient northern China: (i) from Di (狄), the name of an ancient state (thought to have been located in Hebei or Shandong province), which was granted to Xiao Bo or Kao Bo by King Cheng of Zhou (reigned 1042–1021  BC ). (ii) adopted as a surname by the Ku Di (庫狄) family during the Northern Wei dynasty (386–534  AD ). (iii) traced back to other minority ethnic groups in ancient northwestern China.

Mandarin form of the Chinese surname 邸, meaning ‘official residence’ in Chinese: (i) from placename Di (邸), the name of a county (unidentified location). (ii) said to be borne by some descendants of Kujula Kadphises (called 丘就郤 or 邸就郤 in Chinese, 30–c. 80 AD ), founder of the Kushan Empire (one branch of the Yuezhi people originating from western China) located in Central Asia during the Eastern Han dynasty (25–220 AD ).

variants.name: Chi
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Dong, Yun, Cheng, Jae, Yong, Cheng Chung, Chi, Chun, Duk, Fang, Feng.  Korean Chung, Chong, Jae Hong, Moon, Myong, Pong, Shen, Yoo, Byung, Chang, Chol, Chong Hun.  Southeast Asian Phi, Hung, Ly, Tu, Denh, Do Van, Duong, Hao, Kieu, Mui, Nga, Phat.

Chinese: Mandarin and Cantonese form of the surname 池, meaning ‘moat’ in ancient Chinese: (i) acquired as a surname by people who lived beside a moat. (ii) from Chi (池), said to be the name of an ancient state (of unknown location and period). (iii) from the personal name Chi (池), personal name of a son of an official in the state of Qin during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ).

Chinese: Mandarin and Cantonese form of the surname 遲, meaning ‘late’ in Chinese: (i) borne by descendants of Chi Ren (遲任), a person who lived during the Shang dynasty (1600–1046  BC ). (ii) shortened form of the Chinese compound surname Yu-Chi (尉遲), originally the name of a tribe from the Xianbei ethnic group in northern China. (iii) from the second element of the personal name Zi Chi (子遲), style name of Fan Xu, a student of Confucius (551–479  BC ).

nameMeanings.name: Tricarico
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Rocco, Vito, Donato, Angelo, Carlo, Gilda, Mauro, Pasquale, Sal, Santo, Serafino.
Italian (southern): habitational name from Tricarico in Matera province, named after the Latin personal name Tricarius.

nameMeanings.name: Giacomo
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament. See James .

variants.name: James
meanings: form of the name borne in the New Testament by two of Christ's disciples, James son of Zebedee and James son of Alphaeus. This form comes from Late Latin Iacomus, a variant of Iacobus, Latin form of Greek Iakobos. This is the same name as Old Testament Jacob (Hebrew Yaakov), but for many centuries now they have been thought of in the English-speaking world as two distinct names. In Britain, James is a royal name that from the beginning of the 15th century onwards was associated particularly with the Scottish house of Stewart: James I of Scotland ( 1394–1437 ; ruled 1424–37 ) was a patron of the arts and a noted poet, as well as an energetic ruler. King James VI of Scotland ( 1566–1625 ; reigned 1567–1625 ) succeeded to the throne of England in 1603 . His grandson, James II of England ( 1633–1701 ; reigned 1685–8 ) was a Roman Catholic, deposed in 1688 in favour of his Protestant daughter Mary and her husband William of Orange. From then on he, his son (also called James), and his grandson Charles (‘Bonnie Prince Charlie’) made various unsuccessful attempts to recover the English throne. Their supporters were known as Jacobites (from Latin Iacobus), and the name James became for a while particularly associated with Roman Catholicism on the one hand, and Highland opposition to the English government on the other. Nevertheless, it has since become one of the most perennially popular boys' names.

en.lifeSummary: When Giacomo Sanseverino  conte di Tricarico was born about 1170, in San Severino, Celle di Bulgheria, Salerno, Campania, Italy, his father, Roger le Vieux Sanseverino Conte di Tricarico, was 16 and his mother, Rogasia de Tricarico, was 16. He married Maria Albiria de Sicile. They were the parents of at least 1 son. He died before 1216, in Puglia, Italy.

Il a épousé Maria Albiria de Sicile. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils. Il est décédé avant 1216, à Puglia, Italie.
